coredata.dc:title: Electrospinning membranes with Au@carbon dots: Low toxicity and efficient antibacterial photothermal therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Hua
Hong, Jixuan
Li, Changgui
Qiu, Yuheng
Li, Mengting
Qin, Ziyu
Ghiladi, Reza A.
Yin, Xueqiong
coredata.dc:description: As bacterial infections continue to pose a significant challenge to healthcare globally, new therapeutic strategies, interventions, and complementary approaches that address both infection prevention and treatment are needed. As one such strategy, photothermal therapy (PTT) as a non-chemotherapeutic approach is considered a safe and potentially efficient strategy to combat bacterial infections, particularly for antibiotic-resistant pathogens given that PTT operates via a temperature-dependent process against which the development of bacterial resistance is unlikely. Here, we prepared Au@CDs composite nanoparticles (Au@CD) comprised of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and carbon dots (N,S-CDs), and investigated their use as a photothermal agent in PTT. The presence of the CDs as surface decorations conferred improved photothermal conversion efficiency, photostability, and biocompatibility to the Au@CD when compared to the parent AuNPs. To investigate if the Au@CD could serve as a PTT wound dressing and accelerate tissue repair, they were embedded within a PVA membrane via electrospinning. The resultant Au@CD membrane exhibited excellent biocompatibility and photothermal antimicrobial activity. In vitro photothermal antibacterial inactivation studies confirmed their efficacy against S. aureus and E. coli (99 + % inactivation of both pathogens under NIR irradiation). Moreover, in vivo studies employing Kunming male mice with S. aureus-infected wounds on their backs were chosen as a trauma model, with the Au@CD membranes serving as wound dressings. The results showed that a local temperature increased up to 50 °C upon NIR irradiation could effectively eradicate bacteria at the wound site, reduce the risk of bacterial infection, suppress inflammation as well as improve collagen deposition and angiogenesis, all of which together facilitated wound closure and resulted in a better therapeutic effect than the controls. Taken together, this work confirms that NIR-irradiated Au@CD-based membranes and related materials are promising photothermal antimicrobial platforms for wound dressings and related healthcare applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Antibacterial
Carbon dots
Gold nanoparticles
Photothermal
Wound dressings

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Evaluation of wound healing in vivo
Formulation optimization of the composite nanoparticles
Morphology and structure of the Au@CD composite nanoparticles
Photothermal properties of the Au@CD composite nanoparticles
Preparation and characterization of Au@CD membranes
In vitro cytotoxicity of the Au@CD membrane
In vivo wound healing in a S. aureus-infected trauma model

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) DLS measurements of Au@CD (the mass ratios of N,S-CDs:AuNPs were 0.5:1, 1:1, 1.5:1, 2:1, respectively). (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of the resultant Au@CD. Inset: the UV–vis absorption spectrum of N,S-CDs.
TEM (top) and HRTEM (bottom) images of (a) N,S-CDs, (b) AuNPs, and (c) Au@CD. (d) EDXS elemental mapping images of Au@CD.
(a) The XPS survey scan of N,S-CDs and Au@CD. The high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) O1s and (c) C1s for the N,S-CDs, and the spectra of (d) Au4f, (e) O1s and (f) C1s of the Au@CD.

(a) Thermal infrared images and (b) temperature of N,S-CDs, AuNPs and Au@CD under NIR laser irradiation (808 nm, 3 W·cm−2) as a function of irradiation time. (c) Photothermal stability of the Au@CD under 808 nm laser irradiation at 3 W·cm−2 for 2 min repeated ten times.

(a) SEM images of PVA, AuNPs and Au@CD membranes with different formulations (VAu@CD/VPVA = 0.3, 0.6, 1.0, 1.5). The scale bar is 3 μm and 1 μm, respectively. (b) Thermal infrared images of PVA control, AuNPs and Au@CD membranes under 808 nm NIR laser irradiation at two different light intensities.

Antimicrobial activities of the various membranes described in Fig. 5 in the presence and absence of 808 nm NIR laser irradiation. Representative agar plate images, and corresponding viability by colony counting for E. coli (a, c) and S. aureus (b, d). *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01.

SEM (a, b) and confocal fluorescence microscopy (c, d) images of E. coli and S. aureus, respectively, as a function of 808 nm NIR laser irradiation. The scale bars of confocal fluorescence microscopy images were 5 μm.
Cell viability of the N,S-CDs (a) and different membranes (b) incubated with L929 cells for 24 h. *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01.

(a) Schematic illustration of creating a bacterial skin infection on the back of a mouse and its treatment. (b) Infrared thermograph of the Au@CD membrane implant site after 5 min NIR irradiation with a 808 nm laser. (c) Photographs of the S. aureus-infected wounds taken on the 1st, 3rd and 10th days after various treatments as indicated. (d) Wound area healing progression at different healing times.

Images of mice wound tissue sections at Day 10 after no treatment (control) and with Au@CD membrane treatment in the absence and presence of NIR irradiation. Images were visualized by (a) H&E and Masson's staining, and (b) immunofluorescence staining of CD31 (red) and nuclei (DAPI, blue), respectively. Scale bar is 20 μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of the preparation of the photothermal antibacterial Au@CD membrane.

head.ce:title: Electrospinning membranes with Au@carbon dots: Low toxicity and efficient antibacterial photothermal therapy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Tropical Biological Resources of Ministry of Education
Key Lab for Marine Drugs of Haikou

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: R. A. Ghiladi, Department of Chemistry, North Carolina State University, Raleigh, NC, 27695-8204, USA.
Correspondence to: X. Yin, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Hainan University, 58th Renmin Road, Haikou, Hainan 570228, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As bacterial infections continue to pose a significant challenge to healthcare globally, new therapeutic strategies, interventions, and complementary approaches that address both infection prevention and treatment are needed. As one such strategy, photothermal therapy (PTT) as a non-chemotherapeutic approach is considered a safe and potentially efficient strategy to combat bacterial infections, particularly for antibiotic-resistant pathogens given that PTT operates via a temperature-dependent process against which the development of bacterial resistance is unlikely. Here, we prepared Au@CDs composite nanoparticles (Au@CD) comprised of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and carbon dots (N,S-CDs), and investigated their use as a photothermal agent in PTT. The presence of the CDs as surface decorations conferred improved photothermal conversion efficiency, photostability, and biocompatibility to the Au@CD when compared to the parent AuNPs. To investigate if the Au@CD could serve as a PTT wound dressing and accelerate tissue repair, they were embedded within a PVA membrane via electrospinning. The resultant Au@CD membrane exhibited excellent biocompatibility and photothermal antimicrobial activity. In vitro photothermal antibacterial inactivation studies confirmed their efficacy against S. aureus and E. coli (99 + % inactivation of both pathogens under NIR irradiation). Moreover, in vivo studies employing Kunming male mice with S. aureus-infected wounds on their backs were chosen as a trauma model, with the Au@CD membranes serving as wound dressings. The results showed that a local temperature increased up to 50 °C upon NIR irradiation could effectively eradicate bacteria at the wound site, reduce the risk of bacterial infection, suppress inflammation as well as improve collagen deposition and angiogenesis, all of which together facilitated wound closure and resulted in a better therapeutic effect than the controls. Taken together, this work confirms that NIR-irradiated Au@CD-based membranes and related materials are promising photothermal antimicrobial platforms for wound dressings and related healthcare applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infections have emerged as a serious global healthcare challenge that threatens both human health and quality of life, particularly in light of a recent study that suggests that deaths attributable to antibiotic-resistant pathogens will claim 10 million lives annually by 2050, surpassing all cancer-related deaths [1]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop effective strategies that are capable of treating and preventing pathogenic infections, including those that are antibiotic-resistant. To this end, a significant number of studies focusing on both chemotherapeutic (antibiotics [2], antimicrobial peptides [3], quaternary ammonium compounds [4], cationic materials [5], metal and metal oxides [6]) and non-chemotherapeutic (photodynamic therapy [7] and photothermal therapy [8]) strategies have been evaluated. In particular, photothermal therapy (PTT) as a non-chemotherapeutic approach is considered a safe and potentially efficient strategy to combat bacterial infections. PTT exhibits a number of tremendous advantages over other strategies, including the ability to inactivate pathogens via a temperature-dependent process (against which the development of bacterial resistance is unlikely), it can destroy the structure of bacterial biofilms that often pose challenges to more traditional antibiotic treatments, and is considered environmentally friendly [9]. PTT utilizes near-infrared (NIR) light in the phototherapeutic window ranging from 700 to 1100 nm that is ideally suited for deep tissue penetration, with low absorption by hemoglobin and water, and results in minimal damage to healthy tissues [10,11]. Despite these advantages, however, the use of PTT for the prevention and treatment of bacterial infections remains in its infancy, with significantly more studies being needed to identify photothermal agents that are both safe and effective for a wide variety of in vitro and in vivo applications.

In PTT, photothermal agents (PTAs) are the primary components responsible for killing bacteria by transforming light energy into heat that can rupture cell membranes and denature proteins leading to irreversible bacterial damage. Many PTAs have been investigated, including carbon-, germanium-, silver-, and gold-based nanomaterials [12]. Recently, gold-based nanomaterials have aroused significant interest in the field of photothermal antibacterial therapy due to their remarkable surface plasmon resonance (SPR) properties that can generate sufficiently high temperatures to kill bacteria, thus reducing infection and promoting healing processes [13,14]. However, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) often possess an unacceptable degree of toxicity, which limits their application in biomedicine [15]. An additional challenge to the use of AuNPs in PTT is the difficulty in maintaining obtaining efficient heat generation due to the alteration in nanoparticle surface morphology and nanostructure caused by melting during prolonged laser irradiation [16]. To overcome these challenges, surface decoration on Au nanoparticles to obtain Au-based nanocomposites have increasingly gained attention in antibacterial PTT as the means to improve both the biocompatibility and photothermal stability of pure AuNPs. Although several inorganic compounds have been composited with AuNPs, the toxicity and photostability of Au-based nanoparticles remain significant issues. Therefore, further studies are needed to overcome the aforementioned challenges [17,18].

As one possible solution, carbon dots (CDs) have shown promise as inorganic nanoparticles in biomedical applications due to their excellent biocompatibility, good hydrophilicity, high photostability, and facile synthesis [19–21]. Moreover, carbon-based nanomaterials have been shown to increase the photothermal conversion property of AuNPs while conferring minimal cytotoxicity [22,23]. Huang et al. reported that carbon quantum dots could improve the biocompatibility and reduce the cytotoxicity of ZnFe2O4 [24]. Yu et al. prepared hollow-structured CuS nanoparticles composited with carbon dots (CuSCDs), which showed excellent photothermal conversion efficiency under NIR irradiation, as well as enhanced biocompatibility and reduced toxicity [25]. Therefore, we hypothesized that CDs are a promising candidate for AuNPs decoration to improve their biocompatibility, photothermal conversion efficiency, and photothermal stability.

To address this hypothesis, here we prepared Au@CDs composite nanoparticles (Au@CD) via surface modification of AuNPs with CDs (Scheme 1 ), and examined their physical (SEM, TEM, XRD) and photothermal properties. In order to investigate their potential to prevent bacterial infections as additives in wound dressing applications, electrospinning was employed to embed the Au@CD composite nanoparticles with a polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) membrane. The resultant Au@CD membrane was investigated through photothermal antibacterial activity in vitro against both Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Gram-negative Escherichia coli (E. coli), and its mechanism of pathogen inactivation assessed through a combination of live/dead staining assays and scanning electron microscopy. To further assess the photothermal antibacterial efficacy and healing ability of the Au@CD membrane as a potential wound dressing under NIR light irradiation in vivo, Kunming male mice with S. aureus-infected wounds on their backs were chosen as a trauma model. As will be presented, both the in vitro and in vivo studies strongly validate our hypothesis that the Au@CD membrane exhibits good biocompatibility and photothermal antibacterial activity, and suggest that such photothermal agents may have potential applications as additives in bandages and dressings for the prevention and/or treatment of bacterial wound infections.

Chitosan (CS, deacetylation degree ≥95 %, Aladdin Ltd.). Polyvinyl alcohol 224 (PVA, viscosity 40.0–48.0 mPa·s) and Ethylenediamine (EDA) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Gold (III) chloride hydrate (HAuCl4) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4) were purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory (Guangzhou, China). All other reagents used in the experiment were analytical grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water was used throughout the experiment.

6 mL ethylenediamine and 0.4 g CS were added into a beaker, and stirred for 15 min, after which 4 mL of concentrated sulfuric acid was slowly added dropwise. After the reaction was cooled to room temperature, 20 mL of distilled water was added, and the mixture was centrifuged (9900 ×g, 20 min). The supernatant was subsequently, collected and precipitated with 250 mL ethyl alcohol for 12 h. The mixture was filtered, the filtrate was concentrated by rotary evaporation, and finally dried in an oven at 60 °C. The N,S-CDs (0.70 g) were dispersed in the ultrapure water as stock solution (10 mg·mL−1) for further study.

Chitosan (400 mg) was added into a round-bottom flask, and dissolved in 20 mL 1 % acetic acid solution, then 1 mL 1 % HAuCl4 solution was added with stirring at room temperature for 10 min, after which the reaction mixture was heated at 95 °C for 1 h. The solution turned pale yellow to wine red during this time. The reaction was cooled to room temperature and filtered with a 0.22 μm filter, the AuNPs solution was obtained.

4 mL of N,S-CDs solutions at variable concentrations (1.25 mg·mL−1, 2.50 mg·mL−1, 3.75 mg·mL−1, 5.00 mg·mL−1) were mixed with 1 mL AuNPs solution. The reaction mixtures were stirred at room temperature in the dark for 8 h, followed by dialysis against deionized water for 36 h (500–1000 Da). The obtained Au@CD solutions were filtered (0.22 μm) prior to storage.

20.0 g PVA was added into a round-bottom flask, dispersed in 80 mL water and heated to 90 °C with stirring to completely dissolve the PVA. A series of electrospun solutions were obtained by mixing 20 % PVA with Au@CD in the proportions of 1:0, 1:0.3, 1:0.6, 1:1, and 1:1.5 under vigorous stirring for 12 h. The AuNPs content of the AuNPs membrane is the same as that of the Au@CD membrane (VAu@CD/VPVA = 1.0).

The electrospun membrane was prepared on a DT-200 electrospinning machine (China) using the following parameters: the feeding speed of the solution in the syringe was set at 0.5 mL·h−1 by a syringe pump, the implemented voltage of 20.0 kV was applied, and the distance between the tip of the needle and the collector was 15 cm [26].

Particle size and zeta potential measurements were performed by photon correlation spectroscopy employing a Nano ZS90 laser particle analyzer (Malvern Instrument, UK). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were observed on a JEM-2100 microscope under an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were obtained by field emission SEM (HITACHI, S-4800). A PHI5000 Versaprobe-II spectrometer with a monochromatic Al-Kα (1486.6 eV) source was applied to collect X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data. Crystallinity was determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD) with a Bruker D8 instrument using Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.541 Å) in the 2θ ranging from 10° to 80°. UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained using a TU-1901 UV–vis spectrophotometer (China). Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded on a F-320 fluorescence spectrophotometer (China). Au content in Au@CD was determined by an inductive coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES, Agilent ICPOES730, America).

The Au@CD were assessed as a photothermal agent for PTT by monitoring the temperature increase of a Au@CD solution (10 mg·mL−1) under 808 nm NIR laser (LR-MFJ-808) irradiation using a Testo 871 infrared thermal imager. Additional controls and studies were performed on the following: a water-only solution, N,S-CDs (10 mg·mL−1), AuNPs (10 mg·mL−1), PVA membrane, AuNPs membrane, and Au@CD membranes following the same process for the Au@CD solution.

In vitro photothermal antibacterial studies employing the Au@CD membrane against ATCC25923 S. aureus and ATCC25922 E. coli were investigated. All the samples were processed using the same way. Membranes with a diameter of 6 mm were placed at the bottom of 96-well plates, and 80 μL bacterial solution (1 × 107 CFU·mL−1) were added to each plate. The solution was incubated at 37 °C for 2 h and illuminated with/without 808 nm laser at 3 W·cm−2 for 10 min. Thereafter, the antibacterial properties of the different membranes were also studied by agar plate colony counting. A bacterial suspension of each group was diluted 100-fold, and 50 μL of the dilutions were spread onto LB agar plates to grow for 24 h at 37 °C. The colonies were counted at 24 h, and each assay was carried out in triplicate.

Bacteria staining with propidium iodide was also employed to visualize the inactivated cells. After the above 808 nm NIR laser irradiation step, the sample membranes were co-stained with 20 μL (100 μg·mL−1) propidium iodide (PI) for 20 min in the dark. After washing with PBS for 3 times, the dead bacteria were observed using a LEICA SP8 laser scanning confocal microscope.

The morphological characterization of the inactivated bacteria was assessed by SEM. After the sample membranes were illuminated by the 808 nm NIR laser, the bacteria were collected by centrifugation (9900 ×g, 15 min) and fixed with 200 μL 2.5 % glutaraldehyde overnight at 4 °C. The bacteria samples were then dehydrated gradually by 200 μL of increasing ethanol gradient concentrations (30 %, 50 %, 70 %, 80 %, 85 %, 90 %, 95 %, and 100 % ethanol), dried at 37 °C, and gold-sputtered prior to SEM observation.

To evaluate the cytocompatibility of the Au@CD membranes (6 mm), CCK-8 analysis was carried out to evaluate their cytotoxicity in vitro. Briefly, L929 cells with an average density of 1.0 × 105 cells·mL−1 were seeded in a 96-well microplate with complete medium. After 24 h of incubation (37 °C, 5 % CO2), corresponding samples were put to each well of the plate. After another 24 h incubation, 10 μL CCK-8 solution was added to each well and incubated for an additional 2 h. Negative and positive controls were cells treated with pure media and AuNPs membranes, respectively. Finally, the OD450 of each well was measured to determine the cell viability. Cell viability was calculated according to Eq. (1):
(1)
Cell viability
%
=
A S
−
A 0
A C
−
A 0
× 100 %
where As, Ac, A0 is the absorbance of the sample, control and blank medium, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of wound healing in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: All animal experiments were approved by the Animal Welfare and Ethical Review of Hainan University (Hainan, China) with the approval number of HNUAUCC-2021-00081. The wound model was built on the back skin of Kunming male mice by punching a hole with a diameter of ∼8 mm. After inoculating the wound with 100 μL S. aureus solution (108 CFU·mL−1), the Au@CD membrane was used to cover the wound. The mice were then divided into two groups in equal number, and 808 nm NIR laser irradiation was applied to one group for 5 min, with the other group serving as a NIR irradiation-free control for comparative purposes. Photographs of the wound areas were taken on the 1st, 3rd and 10th days post-treatment. Finally, all mice were sacrificed for H&E staining, Masson's trichrome staining, and CD31 immunofluorescence staining [27,28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formulation optimization of the composite nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The photothermal effects produced by photothermal reagents are induced by surface plasmon resonance (SPR), with the particle size and electron charge density being two contributing factors [29]. Therefore, the ratio between the N,S-CDs and the AuNPs was optimized as a function of both particle size and SPR. The DLS results shown in Table S1 revealed that the hydrodynamic size of the Au@CD increased from 69.1 nm to 103.7 nm as the mass ratio of N,S-CDs:AuNPs increased from 0.5:1 to 2:1 (Fig. 1a). This phenomenon could result from the adsorption of the N,S-CDs onto the AuNPs surface due to their opposite zeta potentials: AuNPs (33.3 ± 1.2 mV) vs. N,S-CDs (−4.1 ± 1.0 mV). In addition, the trend in decreasing zeta potential of the Au@CD (Table S1) upon increasing N,S-CDs content is consistent with the self-assembly between the AuNPs and N,S-CDs as governed by electrostatic interactions.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of the AuNPs, N,S-CDs, and Au@CD are shown in Fig. 1b, with the features at 276 and 329 nm in the inset being ascribed to the π → π* transition of CC and the n → π* transition of CO [30], respectively. Previous studies have shown that the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) absorbance in AuNPs exhibit a characteristic peak near 520 nm [31]. Here, we observed the characteristic SPR peak in the Au@CD as being red-shifted to ∼530 nm, with significant broadening likely due to the increasing particle size of the photothermal nanoparticles and the enhancement of plasma coupling among these particles [23,32]. Specifically, we note a greater red-shift in the SPR peak as a function of the increasing N,S-CDs:AuNPs mass ratio, which correlates with larger particle size. In general, the exact position of the plasma band is sensitive to the size and the shape of the particles, as well as the optical and electronic properties of the medium surrounding the particles [33]. The redshift of the SPR absorption for Au@CD can be attributed to two facts. First, the larger particle size of the Au@CD would induce a greater plasma coupling effect. Second, the carbon coatings on AuNPs could increase the effective dielectric constant due to the higher refractive index of carbon (1.6–2.0) than that of water (1.33) [34]. Of note, the SPR red-shift was not as pronounced between the Au@CD of mass ratios 1.5:1 vs. 2:1, which could be attributed to the similar sizes between these two samples (Table S1), implying they likely possess a similar performance in photothermal applications [35]. As such, Au@CD with the mass ratio of 1.5:1 was selected for further use in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure of the Au@CD composite nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The sizes and surface morphologies of the N,S-CDs, AuNPs and Au@CD were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). The N,S-CDs were uniformly dispersed and spherical (Fig. 2a), with the HRTEM revealing clear lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.20 nm, belonging to the diffraction plane of graphite carbon (100) [36]. The AuNPs exhibited a monodisperse state (Fig. 2b), with an interplanar spacing of 0.24 nm that matches with the (111) plane of Au [37]. The Au@CD tended to form chain-like nanostructures (Fig. 2c), which were most likely formed as a result of electrostatic interactions between the AuNPs and N,S-CDs due to their opposite zeta potentials (Table S1). The Au content in Au@CD was 1.5 wt%, which was determined via ICP-OES. In addition, C, N, S, and Au were evenly distributed on the Au@CD according to the mapping results (Fig. 2d), which further proved the successful combination of N,S-CDs and AuNPs.

The chemical composition analysis of the prepared Au@CD was evaluated by XPS (Fig. 3 ). As expected, the XPS survey scan confirmed the presence of Au, S, C, N, O (Fig. 3a) [38,39]. To further understand the changes in chemical species before and after the assembly of the N,S-CDs and AuNPs, the C1s, O1s, and Au4f spectra of the N,S-CDs and Au@CD were de-convoluted. For the N,S-CDs, the O1s spectrum showed the peaks at 531.7, 533.4 eV (Fig. 3b), corresponding to COC/COH and CO. The C1s spectrum showed the peaks at 284.8, 286, 288.7 eV (Fig. 3c), which could be assigned to CC/CC, CO/CN/C-S and CO [36]. For the Au@CD, two peaks could be found in the Au4f spectrum (Fig. 3d), belonging to Au4f7/2 (83.2 eV) and 4f5/2 (87.1 eV), respectively [40,41]. Meanwhile, the peaks in the O1s and C1s spectra of the Au@CD (Fig. 3e and Fig. 3f) were similar to those observed for the N,S-CDs, but there was an obvious peak-shift that suggested an interaction between the AuNPs and the N,S-CDs. Specifically, the lower COC/COH ratio in O1s after the assembly of the N,S-CDs and AuNPs could be attributed to the AuNPs replacing oxygen on the surface of the N,S-CDs. Furthermore, for the Au@CD, the electrons on AuNPs transfer to N,S-CDs, resulting in an increase in charge density on the surface of N,S-CDs and a red shift (low energy shift) of the CO signal in the O1s and C1s [42]. The O1s and C1s results together suggest that the binding site between the AuNPs and the N,S-CDs is likely CO. In addition, the lower energy and broader peak in the Au@CD demonstrate the higher charge density and wider energy distribution of valence electrons on the surface of Au@CD [43,44], both of which enhance light absorption at longer wavelengths (as shown in Fig. 1b), resulting in a stronger SPR effect, which is beneficial to their performance in photothermal applications.

Fig. S1 shows the XRD patterns for the AuNPs, N,S-CDs and Au@CD. The broad diffraction peak of the N,S-CDs at 24.3° was assigned to the (002) plane of graphite, indicating its presence in this material [45]. The AuNPs exhibited diffraction peaks at 38.3°, 44.4°, corresponding to the (111) and (200) crystal planes of Au, respectively [46]. For the composite Au@CD material, diffraction peaks were noted at 24.3°, 38.3°, and 44.4°, which are a combination of those from the N,S-CDs and AuNPs. We note that the AuNPs appear to maintain their original structure even after being modified by the N,S-CDs. These XRD results, when viewed alongside those above for the TEM and XPS studies, strongly indicated the successful preparation of the Au@CD from the surface modification of AuNPs by the N,S-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal properties of the Au@CD composite nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the photothermal properties of the Au@CD in solution (10 mg·mL−1), temperatures were monitored as a function NIR irradiation. As shown in Fig. 4a and b, the temperature of the Au@CD solution increased to 52 °C upon 808 nm irradiation with a 3 W·cm−2 laser for 2 min, far greater than that observed for the water control (31 °C), N,S-CDs solution (30 °C), or AuNPs solution (38 °C). These results demonstrate that the N,S-CDs modification enhances the photothermal conversion capacity (i.e., photothermal amplification) of the AuNPs. Furthermore, the photothermal performance stability was investigated through several cycles of the repetitive irradiation and cooling (Fig. 4c). The conjugation of CDs on AuNPs improved the plasmonic coupling effect and resulted in a high degree of thermal stability [23,47]. Together the results indicate that the Au@CD possess superior photothermal conversion ability and thermal stability when compared to AuNPs alone, suggesting that Au@CD may have a significant advantage for PTT in the treatment of bacterial infections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of Au@CD membranes
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the Au@CD composite nanoparticles in photothermal wound healing applications, wound dressing membranes comprised of different formulations of Au@CD nanoparticles within polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) were prepared by electrospinning (Fig. 5a), and compared with similarly-prepared PTA-free (PVA alone) and AuNPs membranes. All the electrospun membranes displayed well-defined fiber morphologies with smooth fiber surfaces (Fig. 5a). When the N,S-CD, AuNPs and Au@CD membranes were irradiated under 808 nm NIR laser irradiation for 10 min at 1.5 W·cm−2 and 3 W·cm−2, thermal infrared images showed that the Au@CD membrane exhibited excellent photothermal conversion ability (Fig. 5b). Especially for the 3 W·cm−2 study, the control and AuNPs membranes showed an increase in temperature to 42–49 °C, whereas the Au@CD membranes exhibited a much greater increase in temperature (to 50–64 °C) that was positively correlated to the concentration of AuCDs within the PVA membrane. The temperature elevation curves of the PVA, AuNPs and Au@CD membranes under 808 nm NIR laser irradiation at 3 W·cm−2 are depicted in Fig. S2. The data show that the initial heating rate of PVA alone (PTA-free control) was 16 °C·min−1, whereas those of the Au@CD membranes (VAu@CD/VPVA = 0.3, 0.6, 1.0, 1.5) were 26 °C·min−1, 38 °C·min−1, 46 °C·min−1 and 54 °C·min−1, respectively. When compared with the AuNPs membrane that exhibited a heating rate of 19.5 °C·min−1, it is clear that the presence of the N,S-CDs in the Au@CD membrane significantly enhances the photothermal performance of the AuNPs. Moreover, the data showed that the photothermal effect of the Au@CD observed in solution could be replicated within a PVA wound dressing membrane, suggesting that the Au@CD membrane could potentially exhibit excellent NIR performance for photothermal therapy against bacterial infections.

Both Gram-negative E. coli and Gram-positive S. aureus bacteria were used to evaluate the antibacterial activities of the Au@CD membranes with and without NIR laser irradiation (Fig. 6 ). Agar plate growth studies (Fig. 6a and b) were performed, and their corresponding viability by colony counting (Fig. 6c and d) was determined. The results showed that the greater bacterial inactivation as a function of increasing Au@CD content within the membranes (VAu@CD/VPVA = 0.3, 0.6, 1.0, 1.5) was observed under NIR irradiation, reaching upwards of 100 % inactivation of both pathogens. No statistically significant inactivation of either bacterium was observed for the PVA (PTA-free control) membrane. Although the AuNPs membrane demonstrated E. coli and S. aureus inactivation effectiveness, it was still lower than that observed for Au@CD membranes, suggesting the improvement in photothermal antibacterial activity conferred by the Au@CD. Given its overall superior antibacterial performance, the Au@CD membrane with the VAu@CD/VPVA = 1.5 formulation was selected for the subsequent experiments.

The morphological changes of the bacteria as a function of photothermal treatment were investigated by SEM (Fig. 7 ). In the absence of NIR irradiation, both E. coli and S. aureus exhibited intact and smooth cell morphologies, regardless of the membrane composition, i.e., identical morphologies were observed for the PVA (as a PTA-free control), AuNPs, and Au@CD membranes. After exposure to NIR light, the surface morphology changes of both bacteria treated with the PVA membrane were negligible, demonstrating that light itself is not able to inactivate the pathogens. The bacteria treated with the AuNPs membrane and NIR light showed a partial collapse of the cell surface, consistent with the low level of inactivation observed in Fig. 6. Of note, however, the bacteria treated with a combination of the Au@CD membrane and NIR light exhibited extensively wrinkled cell surfaces with a broken appearance and concave holes, indicating the greater photothermal properties of the Au@CD membrane led to significant damage of the bacterial cell [48] consistent with the high level of pathogen inactivation shown in Fig. 6.

To further investigate the mechanism of pathogen inactivation by the Au@CD membrane under NIR light irradiation, a propidium iodide (PI) fluorescent staining assay was used to evaluate cell membrane integrity by confocal laser scanning fluorescence microscopy (CLSM). PI is a membrane impermeable dye that can only fluoresce with compromised bacterial cell membranes at an excitation/emission wavelength of 535/617 nm [49]. The bacteria that exhibit a red fluorescence are considered dead, and thus PI staining results can visually display the antibacterial efficiency of the as-prepared nanofiber membranes [50]. Fig. 7c and d show that in the absence of NIR light, the presence of any of the three membranes (PVA alone, AuNPs, or Au@CD) led to a low background level of dead bacteria being observed. Under NIR irradiation, no significant increase in the dead bacterial population was seen for the PVA membrane, whereas a slight increase in the dead population was noted in the presence of the AuNPs membrane. However, NIR irradiation led to a clear and obvious increase in the population of dead bacteria for the Au@CD membrane, consistent with the efficient killing of both E. coli and S. aureus cells observed in Fig. 6. Collectively, the above results indicate that the Au@CD membrane possesses a strong photothermal antibacterial activity under NIR light irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity of the Au@CD membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The viability of L929 fibroblast cells were used to evaluate the biocompatibility and cytotoxicity of the Au@CD membrane (Fig. 8 ). The N,S-CDs had no cytotoxicity to L929 cells (Fig. 8a), even at concentrations upwards of 1000 μg·mL−1. The viability of the L929 cells in the presence of the Au@CD and AuNPs membranes was determined to be 95.8 % and 72.0 %, respectively (Fig. 8b). The lower toxicity of the Au@CD membrane compared to that of the AuNPs membrane is attributed to the presence of the non-cytotoxic N,S-CDs on the surface of AuNPs. The p value of 0.42 for control vs. Au@CD membrane was not significant, indicating the low toxicity of the Au@CD. In general, CDs have excellent biocompatibility [30], thus we surmise that the toxicity of the AuNPs was reduced significantly by the CDs surface decoration, thus improving the biocompatibility of the composite Au@CD membrane over that of the AuNPs membrane alone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo wound healing in a S. aureus-infected trauma model
ce:section.ce:para: To further assess the photothermal antibacterial efficacy and healing ability of the Au@CD membrane under NIR light irradiation in vivo, Kunming male mice with S. aureus-infected wounds on their backs were chosen as a trauma model (Fig. 9 ). According to the size and shape of the wound, we cut the various membranes and implanted the corresponding membrane on the wound (Fig. 9a). After NIR irradiation for 5 min, the temperature at the wound region treated with Au@CD membrane reached ∼50 °C (Fig. 9b). As shown above in Fig. 7, such temperatures can kill bacteria through physical damage while being safe for surrounding tissues [27,51], suggesting that the Au@CD membrane could serve as an effective wound dressing to kill bacteria under NIR irradiation without causing damage to surrounding tissue. The wound area on Days 1, 3 and 10 was determined to assess the healing progress as a function of different treatments (Fig. 9c and d). While the wounds in all treated groups became smaller after 10 days, a faster healing was noted when employing the Au@CD membrane in combination with photothermal therapy of the bacterial infection.

Subsequently, both H&E and Masson's trichrome staining procedures were performed on Day 10 to provide histological evaluations of the mouse dermal wound tissues to examine wound healing inflammation and any potential adverse effects (Fig. 10 ). From H&E staining, a large number of inflammatory cells were present in the infected wound tissue for both the control group and the Au@CD membrane without NIR treatment group, whereas negligible inflammatory cells were distributed in the wound that was treated with the Au@CD membrane and NIR irradiation (Fig. 10a) [52]. The results exhibited by Masson's staining (collagen stained in blue) demonstrated more ordered collagen fibers in the Au@CD membrane with NIR treatment group when compared to the control and Au@CD membrane without NIR groups, which indicates that irradiated Au@CD membrane had a positive effect on wound healing process. Immunofluorescence staining of CD31 was also conducted to assess the extent of angiogenesis in wound healing (Fig. 10b). The amount of CD31-positive microvessels (red) observed in the Au@CD membrane with NIR treatment group was far greater than those of either the control or Au@CD membrane without NIR treatment groups. Taken together, the above results indicated that the Au@CD membrane irradiated with NIR light showed a positive photothermal effect on wound healing.

In summary, composite gold nanoparticles decorated with carbon quantum dots exhibited excellent photothermal conversion efficiency under near-infrared light irradiation. Electrostatic adherence of the N,S-CDs enhanced the photothermal properties and improved the biosafety of the resultant Au@CD composite nanoparticles compared to the parent AuNPs. Gratifyingly, the photothermal antibacterial properties of the Au@CD in solution were maintained even when the composite nanoparticles were incorporated into a PVA membrane via electrospinning, with the Au@CD membrane displaying both good biocompatibility/low cytotoxicity but a high degree of bacterial inactivation against E. coli and S. aureus. In vivo results indicated that the Au@CD membranes therapy demonstrated the most effective photothermal antibacterial performance and played an important role in regulating immune response, collagen accumulation and host angiogenesis in mouse trauma models. Overall, the results suggested that the development of photothermal agents such as Au@CD composite nanoparticles may have significant implications for the treatment of bacterial infectious wound management, especially for use as potential additives in wound dressing applications.

Hua Tian: Methodology, Data curation, Writing - Original draft preparation. Jixuan Hong and Changgui Li: Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology. Yuheng Qiu: Data curation, Visualization. Mengting Li: Supervision, Validation, Writing - Reviewing & Editing. Ziyu Qin: Investigation, Validation. Reza A. Ghiladi: Writing - Review & Editing, Validation. Xueqiong Yin: Conceptualization, Resources, Funding acquisition. All authors have read and approved the final version of this manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Key Science and Technology Program of Haikou City (2021-024), Natural Science Foundation of Hainan Province (GHYF2022001), Young Talents' Science and Technology Innovation Project of Hainan Association for Science and Technology (QCXM202030) and the Hainan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (520QN224).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bioadv.2022.213155.
